title: Puppy Scratching Ears and Shaking Head: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your puppy shaking their head? Learn about ear mites, infections, and when to see a vet in our empathetic veterinary triage guide.
content_html:
TL;DR: Puppy ear scratching and head shaking are common signs of infection or mites that require a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours to prevent worsening pain and swelling.

Why is my puppy scratching their ears and shaking their head?

  • Pawing at the ears and vigorous head shaking are primary indicators of irritation, itchiness, or pain.
  • In young dogs, these symptoms are most frequently associated with ear mites, yeast overgrowth, or bacterial infections.
  • Because puppies are still developing, their ears are sensitive environments where moisture and debris easily become trapped.

Is my puppy's ear discomfort a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low; while uncomfortable, it is rarely a life-threatening emergency.
  • Your puppy requires a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours.
  • If left untreated, the underlying cause can lead to secondary infections or a painful aural hematoma caused by trauma from shaking.

How can photos of my puppy’s ear help a veterinarian with triage?

  • A clear, well-lit photo of the inner ear flap and canal opening helps a professional assess the color and consistency of discharge.
  • Dark debris resembling coffee grounds often suggests ear mites, while yellow or green discharge may indicate a bacterial infection.
  • Seeing the level of redness and swelling helps determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen by a vet.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Head shaking and continual ear scratching are common clinical signs associated with otitis externa and otitis media in puppies. Mite infestations, particularly those of the family Psoroptidae, can cause otitis externa, leading to these signs, along with possible ear droop. Otitis media can present similarly, with affected animals exhibiting head tilting or rotation towards the affected side, and potential self-trauma leading to aural hematoma. Concurrent otitis externa may cause inflammation and abnormal discharge in the external ear canal, with the pinna potentially being painful and malodorous. Facial nerve paralysis (ear droop, lip droop, ptosis, nostril collapse) or Horner's syndrome (miosis, ptosis, enophthalmos, protrusion of the nictitating membrane) may be observed on the affected side due to involvement of cranial nerve VII and sympathetic nerves.

Chapter: Dermatology, Parasitology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 921)

faqs.question: Can I use human ear drops on my puppy?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or over-the-counter cleaners without a vet's approval, as some ingredients can be toxic to dogs or damaging if the eardrum is ruptured.

faqs.question: Are ear mites common in puppies?
faqs.answer: Yes, ear mites are very common in puppies and are highly contagious between pets. If you have other animals, they may also need to be checked.

faqs.question: What happens if I ignore the head shaking?
faqs.answer: Ignoring the symptoms can lead to chronic inflammation, permanent scarring of the ear canal, or a ruptured eardrum, which is much more difficult and expensive to treat.
